title: Reverse list (iterative & recursive)
description: Reverse a singly linked list using both approaches

examples.explanation: Start: prev=None, curr=1, next=2. Step1: Save next=2, set 1.next=None, prev=1, curr=2. Step2: Save next=3, set 2.next=1, prev=2, curr=3. Step3: Save next=4, set 3.next=2, prev=3, curr=4. Step4: Save next=5, set 4.next=3, prev=4, curr=5. Step5: Save next=None, set 5.next=4, prev=5, curr=None. Return prev=5 as new head.
Start: prev=None, curr=10, next=20. Step1: Save next=20, set 10.next=None, prev=10, curr=20. Step2: Save next=None, set 20.next=10, prev=20, curr=None. Return prev=20 as new head.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Iterative Pointer Reversal
approaches.summary: Iterate through the list, reversing the next pointers of each node to point to the previous node. Use three pointers (prev, curr, next) to track nodes and update links without extra data structures.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Reverse Linked List Iteratively
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of iterative pointer reversal to reverse a singly linked list.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using the iterative pointer reversal approach. The intuition is to traverse the list once while reversing the direction of each node's next pointer. We maintain three pointers: prev (initially null, becomes the new head), curr (current node being processed), and next (temporarily stores the next node before we break the link). At each step, we save curr.next to next, then reverse curr.next to point to prev. Then we advance prev to curr and curr to next. When curr becomes null, prev is the new head of the reversed list. This works because we're essentially building the reversed list from the front backward, one node at a time. The algorithm runs in O(n) time since we visit each node once, and O(1) space since we only use three pointers regardless of list size. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the core reversal loop. Common mistakes include not handling empty lists, losing the next pointer before saving it, or incorrect pointer updates. Real code pattern: def reverse_list(head): prev = None; curr = head; while curr: next = curr.next; curr.next = prev; prev = curr; curr = next; return prev

examples.trace: Start: prev=None, curr=1, next=2. Step1: Save next=2, set 1.next=None, prev=1, curr=2. Step2: Save next=3, set 2.next=1, prev=2, curr=3. Step3: Save next=4, set 3.next=2, prev=3, curr=4. Step4: Save next=5, set 4.next=3, prev=4, curr=5. Step5: Save next=None, set 5.next=4, prev=5, curr=None. Return prev=5 as new head.
Start: prev=None, curr=10, next=20. Step1: Save next=20, set 10.next=None, prev=10, curr=20. Step2: Save next=None, set 20.next=10, prev=20, curr=None. Return prev=20 as new head.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Single Node Reversal Step
ladders.desc: The core loop that reverses one node's pointer and advances pointers.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function—the single step that gets repeated for each node. The intuition is that to reverse a node, we need to change its next pointer to point backward instead of forward, but we must first save where it was originally pointing so we don't lose the rest of the list. The algorithm for one step: 1) Save curr.next to a temporary next pointer. 2) Reverse curr.next to point to prev. 3) Advance prev to curr. 4) Advance curr to next. This single step has O(1) time and space since it performs constant operations. This feeds into L0 by being repeated in a loop for all nodes. Common mistakes include trying to access curr.next after changing it, or updating pointers in wrong order. Real code pattern: next_temp = curr.next; curr.next = prev; prev = curr; curr = next_temp

examples.trace: Step1: Save next=2 from curr.next. Step2: Set curr.next to prev (None). Step3: Update prev to curr (1). Step4: Update curr to next (2). Result: prev points to node 1 (which now points to None), curr points to node 2.

Step1: Save next=3 from curr.next. Step2: Set curr.next to prev (1). Step3: Update prev to curr (2). Step4: Update curr to next (3). Result: prev points to node 2 (which now points to 1), curr points to node 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Three-Pointer Traversal with Reversal
ladders.desc: The central technique of using three pointers to traverse and reverse links.
ladders.explanation: This is the core insight: using exactly three pointers (prev, curr, next) to traverse the list while reversing links. The intuition is that we need to know the previous node to point to, the current node to modify, and the next node to move to—all simultaneously. Without all three, we would lose references. The algorithm: Initialize prev=None, curr=head. While curr is not null: store curr.next in next, set curr.next to prev, set prev to curr, set curr to next. This maintains the invariant that prev always points to the head of the partially reversed list. Time is O(n) for traversal, space O(1) for three pointers. This connects to L1 as the loop structure around the single step. Common mistakes include using more than three pointers unnecessarily, or incorrect initialization. Real code pattern: prev, curr = None, head; while curr: next = curr.next; curr.next = prev; prev = curr; curr = next

examples.trace: Init: prev=None, curr=1. Iter1: next=2, 1.next=None, prev=1, curr=2. Iter2: next=3, 2.next=1, prev=2, curr=3. Iter3: next=None, 3.next=2, prev=3, curr=None. Loop ends, prev=3 is new head.
Init: prev=None, curr=A. Iter1: next=B, A.next=None, prev=A, curr=B. Iter2: next=None, B.next=A, prev=B, curr=None. Loop ends, prev=B is new head.

ladders.title: Building Block — Traversing a Linked List with a Pointer
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept: moving through a linked list using a current pointer.
ladders.explanation: This is an intermediate concept needed before reversal: how to traverse a singly linked list. The intuition is that we can only move forward via next pointers, so we need a pointer that starts at the head and moves to each subsequent node. The algorithm: Initialize curr = head. While curr is not null: process curr, then set curr = curr.next. This visits each node exactly once. Time O(n) for n nodes, space O(1) for the pointer. This connects to L2 as the traversal mechanism that the three-pointer technique builds upon. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating curr, or accessing null pointers. Real code pattern: curr = head; while curr: # process curr; curr = curr.next

examples.trace: Start curr=1. Iter1: process node 1, curr=1.next=2. Iter2: process node 2, curr=2.next=3. Iter3: process node 3, curr=3.next=None. Loop ends.
Start curr=A. Iter1: process A, curr=A.next=B. Iter2: process B, curr=B.next=C. Iter3: process C, curr=C.next=D. Iter4: process D, curr=D.next=None. Loop ends.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Modifying Node Pointers
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation: reading and updating the next pointer of a node.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation used in reversal: how to access and change a node's next pointer. In a singly linked list node (typically with attributes like value and next), the next pointer stores the reference to the next node. The intuition is that by modifying next, we change the structure of the list. To access: node.next gives the next node. To modify: node.next = new_reference changes where the node points. This is O(1) time and space. This connects to L3 as the basic action performed during traversal. Common mistakes include null pointer errors when accessing next on a null node, or assigning wrong values. Real code pattern: next_node = current_node.next; current_node.next = previous_node

examples.input: Node A with A.next = B, set A.next = C
examples.trace: Initial state: A -> B. Execute: A.next = C. Result: A -> C. Node B is still existent but no longer referenced by A.

examples.input: Node X with X.next = null, set X.next = Y
examples.trace: Initial state: X -> null. Execute: X.next = Y. Result: X -> Y. Y can be any node or null.

testCases.input: NodeFirst.next=NodeSecond, access then set to NodeThird

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Linked List Node?
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: understanding a single node in a singly linked list.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundation concept: a node in a singly linked list. The intuition is that a linked list is built from individual nodes, each containing data and a pointer to the next node. Think of it like a chain where each link knows its value and the next link. A node typically has two attributes: value (or data) storing the element, and next storing a reference to the next node (or null if it's the last node). This is O(1) space per node. This connects to L4 as the object whose pointers we access and modify. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with arrays, or not understanding that next is a reference. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, value): self.value = value; self.next = None

examples.trace: Initialize a Node object. Set its value attribute to 10. Set its next attribute to None (or null). Result: a standalone node not connected to any other node.

examples.input: Create two nodes with values 5 and 15, link first to second
examples.trace: Step1: Create node1 with value=5, next=None. Step2: Create node2 with value=15, next=None. Step3: Set node1.next = node2. Result: node1 points to node2, node2 points to null.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Recursive Stack Reversal
approaches.summary: Recursively traverse to the end of the list, then unwind the call stack to reverse pointers by setting each node's next to its previous node. Base case handles the tail becoming the new head.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Stack Reversal of Linked List
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution to reverse a singly linked list by unwinding the call stack.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages recursion to reverse a linked list by traversing to the end and then rewiring pointers during the return phase. The intuition is that recursion naturally provides a stack-like behavior: we go deep to the tail (base case), then as we return from each recursive call, we have access to both the current node and the previously reversed portion. At each step, we set the current node's next pointer to point to the previous node (which becomes the new head of the reversed list so far), effectively reversing the links. The base case occurs when we reach the end of the list (node is None or node.next is None), at which point we return the current node as the new head. The algorithm: define a recursive function reverse(node) that returns the new head. If node is None or node.next is None, return node (base case). Otherwise, recursively call reverse(node.next) to get the new head. Then set node.next.next = node to reverse the link between node and its next node, and set node.next = None to break the original forward link. Finally, return the new head. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(n) due to the recursion stack depth equal to the list length. This connects to the overall problem by providing a clean, elegant solution that uses the call stack implicitly. Common mistakes include not handling empty lists, forgetting to set node.next to None (causing cycles), or misunderstanding the base case. Real code pattern: def reverse_recursive(head): if not head or not head.next: return head; new_head = reverse_recursive(head.next); head.next.next = head; head.next = None; return new_head.

examples.trace: Step 1: Call reverse(1). Node 1 has next 2, so call reverse(2). Step 2: Call reverse(2). Node 2 has next 3, so call reverse(3). Step 3: Call reverse(3). Node 3 has next 4, so call reverse(4). Step 4: Call reverse(4). Node 4 has next 5, so call reverse(5). Step 5: Call reverse(5). Node 5 has next None, base case returns 5 as new head. Step 6: Return to reverse(4): new_head = 5, set 4.next.next = 4 (5.next = 4), set 4.next = None, return new_head = 5. Step 7: Return to reverse(3): new_head = 5, set 3.next.next = 3 (4.next = 3), set 3.next = None, return new_head = 5. Step 8: Return to reverse(2): new_head = 5, set 2.next.next = 2 (3.next = 2), set 2.next = None, return new_head = 5. Step 9: Return to reverse(1): new_head = 5, set 1.next.next = 1 (2.next = 1), set 1.next = None, return new_head = 5. Final list: 5 -> 4 -> 3 -> 2 -> 1.

Step 1: Call reverse(10). Node 10 has next 20, so call reverse(20). Step 2: Call reverse(20). Node 20 has next None, base case returns 20 as new head. Step 3: Return to reverse(10): new_head = 20, set 10.next.next = 10 (20.next = 10), set 10.next = None, return new_head = 20. Final list: 20 -> 10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Link Rewiring Function
ladders.desc: The recursive function that traverses to the tail and reverses pointers on return.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the recursive reversal approach. The intuition is to use recursion to reach the end of the list, then during the unwinding phase, manipulate pointers to reverse the order. The function takes a node as input and returns the new head of the reversed list from that node onward. The base case handles when we are at the last node or an empty list: if the node is None or its next is None, we return the node itself as it becomes the new head. In the recursive case, we call the function on the next node, which eventually returns the new head from the tail. Then, we rewire pointers: set the current node's next node's next pointer to point back to the current node (node.next.next = node), effectively reversing the link. Finally, we set the current node's next to None to break the original forward link and prevent cycles. The function returns the new head obtained from the recursive call. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing the list, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This feeds into the full problem by providing the core reversal logic that is called on the original head. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling (e.g., missing check for empty list) or not setting node.next to None. Real code pattern: def reverse_node(node): if node is None or node.next is None: return node; new_head = reverse_node(node.next); node.next.next = node; node.next = None; return new_head.

examples.input: Node with value 1, next pointing to 2, and 2 pointing to None
examples.trace: Step 1: Call reverse_node(1). Since 1.next is 2 (not None), call reverse_node(2). Step 2: Call reverse_node(2). Since 2.next is None, base case returns node 2 as new head. Step 3: Return to reverse_node(1): new_head = 2, set 1.next.next = 1 (2.next = 1), set 1.next = None, return new_head = 2. Final: New head is node 2, with 2.next = 1 and 1.next = None.

examples.input: Node with value 5, next pointing to None
examples.trace: Step 1: Call reverse_node(5). Since 5.next is None, base case returns node 5 as new head. No pointer changes needed. Final: New head is node 5, with 5.next = None.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Unwinding Call Stack to Reverse Pointers
ladders.desc: The technique of using return from recursion to set each node's next to its previous node.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the mechanism by which recursion reverses the list during the unwinding phase. The intuition is that recursion builds a call stack as we traverse forward, and when we start returning, we have access to nodes in reverse order. At each return step, we take the current node and the already-reversed portion (from deeper calls) and link them appropriately. Specifically, after the recursive call returns with the new head (which is the original tail), we set the current node's next node's next pointer to point back to the current node. This works because at that moment, the current node's next still points to the node that has already been reversed and is now the predecessor in the new order. By doing node.next.next = node, we make that predecessor point back to the current node, reversing the link. Then, we set node.next = None to detach the original forward link, as the current node will later be pointed to by its new predecessor in the next unwinding step. This process repeats for each node as we unwind the stack, gradually building the reversed list from the tail back to the head. Time and space are O(n) due to stack depth. This connects to the sub-routine by being the essential operation performed after each recursive call. Common mistakes include not understanding the order of operations or incorrectly manipulating pointers. Real code pattern: After recursive call: reversed_part = recursive_call(node.next); node.next.next = node; node.next = None; return reversed_part.

examples.input: At node with value 3 during unwinding, with node.next pointing to 4 and reversed part from 4 onward being 4 -> 5
examples.trace: Step 1: During unwinding, we are at node 3. The recursive call on node 4 returned with reversed part: 5 -> 4 (where 5 is head, 4.next = 5? Wait, let's correct: after reversing from 4, list is 5 -> 4, with 4.next = None initially? Actually, trace: For list 3->4->5, after reverse(4) returns, we have 5->4 with 4.next = None. Then at node 3: set 3.next.next = 3 (4.next = 3), so now 5->4->3, but 4.next was None, now becomes 3. Then set 3.next = None. So final from this step: reversed part becomes 5->4->3 with 3.next = None.

examples.input: At node with value 1 during unwinding, with node.next pointing to 2 and reversed part from 2 onward being 2
examples.trace: Step 1: During unwinding at node 1. Recursive call on node 2 returned with reversed part: 2 (since 2.next was None). Set 1.next.next = 1 (2.next = 1), so now reversed part is 2->1. Set 1.next = None. Final: 2->1 with 1.next = None.

testCases.input: Node A with next B, reversed part from B is B -> C
testCases.expected: After: B.next = A, A.next = None, reversed part becomes B -> C -> A

testCases.input: Node X with next Y, reversed part from Y is Y
testCases.expected: After: Y.next = X, X.next = None, reversed part becomes Y -> X

testCases.input: Node 10 with next 20, reversed part from 20 is 20 -> 30
Node P with next Q, reversed part from Q is Q -> R -> S
testCases.expected: After: Q.next = P, P.next = None, reversed part becomes Q -> R -> S -> P

testCases.input: Node 1 with next 2, reversed part from 2 is 2 -> 3 -> 4
Node alpha with next beta, reversed part from beta is beta
testCases.expected: After: beta.next = alpha, alpha.next = None, reversed part becomes beta -> alpha

testCases.input: Node 100 with next 200, reversed part from 200 is 200 -> 300 -> 400
Node x with next y, reversed part from y is y -> z
testCases.expected: After: y.next = x, x.next = None, reversed part becomes y -> z -> x

testCases.input: Node 5 with next 6, reversed part from 6 is 6 -> 7 -> 8 -> 9
Node M with next N, reversed part from N is N -> O -> P
testCases.expected: After: N.next = M, M.next = None, reversed part becomes N -> O -> P -> M

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Traversal to Tail with Return Value
ladders.desc: Using recursion to reach the end of a linked list and pass back a value.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the concept of recursively traversing a linked list until the tail is reached, and then returning a value (in this case, the tail node) back through the call stack. The intuition is that recursion allows us to break down the problem: we move one node at a time by calling the function on the next node, and when we hit the base case (end of list), we return a result that propagates up. For a singly linked list, the tail is identified when node.next is None. The algorithm: define a function get_tail(node) that returns the tail node. If node is None, return None. If node.next is None, return node (as it is the tail). Otherwise, return get_tail(node.next). This ensures we recursively drill down to the last node and then return it. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse each node once, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. This connects to the core logic by providing the mechanism to reach the tail, which is essential for starting the reversal during unwinding. In the full reversal, we don't just return the tail but use it as the new head. Common mistakes include infinite recursion if base case is incorrect or not handling empty lists. Real code pattern: def get_tail(node): if node is None: return None; if node.next is None: return node; return get_tail(node.next).

examples.trace: Step 1: Call get_tail(1). Since 1.next is 2, call get_tail(2). Step 2: Call get_tail(2). Since 2.next is 3, call get_tail(3). Step 3: Call get_tail(3). Since 3.next is 4, call get_tail(4). Step 4: Call get_tail(4). Since 4.next is 5, call get_tail(5). Step 5: Call get_tail(5). Since 5.next is None, return node 5. Step 6: Return to get_tail(4): return 5. Step 7: Return to get_tail(3): return 5. Step 8: Return to get_tail(2): return 5. Step 9: Return to get_tail(1): return 5. Final output: node 5.
Step 1: Call get_tail(10). Since 10.next is 20, call get_tail(20). Step 2: Call get_tail(20). Since 20.next is None, return node 20. Step 3: Return to get_tail(10): return 20. Final output: node 20.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Pointer Manipulation in Linked List Node
ladders.desc: Changing the next pointer of a node to point to a different node.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves updating the 'next' attribute of a linked list node to reference another node, which is fundamental for modifying the list structure. In a singly linked list, each node has a value and a next pointer that points to the subsequent node. By changing this pointer, we can insert, delete, or reverse nodes. The intuition is that the list's order is determined solely by these pointers, so altering them changes the traversal path. For example, to reverse a link between node A and node B where A.next = B, we can set B.next = A to make B point back to A, and then set A.next to something else (like None or another node) to avoid cycles. The operation is performed using assignment: node.next = new_node. Time complexity is O(1) for a single pointer change, and space is O(1) as no extra memory is needed. This connects to the building block as it is the action taken during recursive unwinding to reverse pointers. In the reversal process, we use node.next.next = node to point the next node back, and node.next = None to clear the forward link. Common mistakes include losing reference to nodes (causing memory leaks or errors) or creating cycles by incorrect assignments. Real code pattern: Given nodes A and B, to reverse link: B.next = A; A.next = None. But note: in context, we often have A.next = B initially, so after B.next = A, we set A.next to None or another node.

examples.input: Node A with value 1, next pointing to B (value 2). Node B next pointing to C (value 3).
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial state: A.next = B, B.next = C. Step 2: Set B.next = A. Now B.next points to A instead of C. Step 3: Set A.next = None. Now A.next is None. Result: Traversal from B goes to A, then stops. C is disconnected unless referenced elsewhere.

examples.input: Node X with value 10, next pointing to Y (value 20). Node Y next pointing to None.
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial state: X.next = Y, Y.next = None. Step 2: Set Y.next = X. Now Y.next points to X. Step 3: Set X.next = None. Now X.next is None. Result: Traversal from Y goes to X, then stops.

testCases.input: A.next = B; set B.next = A; set A.next = None
P.next = Q; set Q.next = P; set P.next = R
M.next = N; set N.next = M; set M.next = None
X.next = Y; set Y.next = X; set X.next = None
alpha.next = beta; set beta.next = alpha; set alpha.next = gamma
a.next = b; set b.next = a; set a.next = c
node1.next = node2; set node2.next = node1; set node1.next = None

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Singly Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understanding a node in a singly linked list with value and next pointer.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the basic building block of a singly linked list: the node. A node is a simple data structure that contains two parts: a value (or data) and a next pointer. The value holds the actual data, such as an integer, string, or object. The next pointer is a reference (or link) to the next node in the sequence. In many programming languages, this is implemented as an object with attributes, e.g., in Python: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.next = None. The intuition is that nodes are connected in a chain via these pointers, forming a list where you can traverse from one node to the next by following the next pointers. The list starts with a head node and ends with a tail node whose next pointer is None, indicating the end. This structure allows dynamic memory allocation and efficient insertions/deletions at the beginning or middle compared to arrays. Time complexity for accessing a node by index is O(n) as you must traverse from the head, but operations at the head are O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for storing n nodes. This connects to the basic operation as it defines what a pointer is and how to manipulate it. For recursion, we use nodes as parameters and return values. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with their values or not initializing next to None. Real code pattern: Define a Node class with val and next attributes.

examples.input: Create a node with value 5 and no next node.
examples.trace: Step 1: Instantiate a Node object, e.g., node = Node(5). Step 2: Set node.val = 5. Step 3: Set node.next = None by default. Result: A single node that can be the head of a list.

examples.input: Create two nodes with values 10 and 20, link them as 10 -> 20.
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node1 = Node(10). Step 2: Create node2 = Node(20). Step 3: Set node1.next = node2. Step 4: node2.next remains None. Result: A linked list with head node1, traversing node1 -> node2.

testCases.input: Node with value 100, next pointing to Node with value 200
Node with value x, next pointing to Node with value y
Node with value A, next pointing to Node with value B, and B next pointing to Node with value C
Node with value abc, next pointing to Node with value def

approaches.name: Approach 3: Stack-Based Reversal
approaches.summary: Push all nodes onto a stack, then pop them to rebuild the list in reverse order. This uses an explicit stack data structure to store nodes temporarily.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Complete Stack-Based Linked List Reversal
ladders.desc: Implement complete iterative stack-based reversal of a singly linked list.
ladders.explanation:
This approach leverages the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) property of stacks to reverse a linked list. The intuition is simple: if you push all nodes onto a stack, then pop them off, they'll come out in reverse order. This works because the stack naturally reverses the sequence without needing pointer manipulation. The algorithm has three phases: 1) Traverse the original list, pushing each node onto a stack. 2) Create a new head from the top of the stack. 3) Pop remaining nodes and link them sequentially. Time complexity is O(n) since we traverse the list twice (push and pop). Space complexity is O(n) for the stack storage. This connects to the parent problem by providing a complete alternative to pointer-based reversal methods. Common mistakes include not handling empty lists, forgetting to set next pointers to null when popping, and memory leaks in languages with manual memory management. The stack approach is particularly useful when you need to preserve the original list structure or when working in environments where pointer manipulation is restricted.

Pseudocode:
function reverseWithStack(head):
    if head is null: return null
    stack = empty stack
    current = head
    while current != null:
        stack.push(current)
        current = current.next
    newHead = stack.pop()
    current = newHead
    while stack is not empty:
        node = stack.pop()
        current.next = node
        current = node
    current.next = null
    return newHead

examples.trace: Step 1: Push nodes to stack. Stack after pushes: [1,2,3] (top=3). Step 2: Pop 3 as new head. newHead=3. Step 3: Pop 2, link 3->2. Step 4: Pop 1, link 2->1. Step 5: Set 1->null. Final: 3->2->1->null.
Step 1: Push 5 to stack. Stack: [5]. Step 2: Pop 5 as new head. newHead=5. Step 3: Stack empty, set 5->null. Final: 5->null.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Stack Reconstruction Phase
ladders.desc: Implement the stack popping and linking phase to rebuild the reversed list.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine focuses on the second half of the reversal process: after nodes are pushed onto a stack, we need to pop them and reconstruct the linked list in reverse order. The intuition is that the stack's LIFO property gives us nodes in exactly the reverse order of insertion. The key insight is that we must carefully manage pointers when popping: each popped node becomes the next node in our new list, and we need to ensure the final node points to null. The algorithm: 1) Pop the first node to create the new head. 2) Set a current pointer to this head. 3) While stack has nodes, pop next node, set current.next to it, move current to this new node. 4) Finally, set current.next to null. Time complexity is O(n) for popping n nodes. Space complexity is O(1) for this phase alone (excluding the stack storage). This feeds into L0 by completing the reversal after the push phase. Common mistakes include forgetting to null-terminate the last node, incorrectly updating pointers, or trying to pop from an empty stack. This phase demonstrates how stack operations naturally create reverse ordering without complex pointer arithmetic.

Pseudocode:
function rebuildFromStack(stack):
    if stack is empty: return null
    newHead = stack.pop()
    current = newHead
    while stack is not empty:
        node = stack.pop()
        current.next = node
        current = node
    current.next = null
    return newHead

examples.trace: Step 1: Pop 3, newHead=3, current=3. Step 2: Pop 2, set 3->2, current=2. Step 3: Pop 1, set 2->1, current=1. Step 4: Set 1->null. Result: 3->2->1->null.
Step 1: Pop 5, newHead=5, current=5. Step 2: Stack empty, set 5->null. Result: 5->null.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Stack Push Phase for List Traversal
ladders.desc: Traverse a linked list and push all nodes onto a stack.
ladders.explanation:
This is the first phase of the stack-based reversal: traversing the original linked list and pushing each node onto a stack. The intuition is that by pushing nodes in their original order, we're storing them in a way that will allow us to retrieve them in reverse order (due to stack's LIFO property). The core technique is simple iteration: start at the head, move through each node using the next pointer, and push each node onto the stack. The key insight is that we're storing references to the nodes themselves, not just their values, so we can later reconnect them. Time complexity is O(n) for n nodes. Space complexity is O(n) for the stack storage. This feeds into L1 by providing the stack that will be used for reconstruction. Common mistakes include pushing node values instead of node objects, not handling empty lists, or infinite loops from incorrect traversal. This phase demonstrates how stack accumulation naturally prepares data for reversal without any explicit reversal logic.

Pseudocode:
function pushNodesToStack(head):
    stack = empty stack
    current = head
    while current != null:
        stack.push(current)
        current = current.next
    return stack

examples.trace: Step 1: current=1, push 1. Stack: [1]. Step 2: current=2, push 2. Stack: [1,2]. Step 3: current=3, push 3. Stack: [1,2,3]. Step 4: current=null, stop. Final stack has 3 on top.
Step 1: current=a, push a. Stack: [a]. Step 2: current=b, push b. Stack: [a,b]. Step 3: current=null, stop. Final stack has b on top.

ladders.title: Building Block — Linked List Traversal with Node Reference Storage
ladders.desc: Traverse a linked list while storing references to each node in a collection.
ladders.explanation:
This intermediate concept involves traversing a linked list and storing references to each node in a data structure (like a list or stack). The intuition is that by keeping references to nodes (not just values), we can later manipulate the list structure. This is crucial for the stack-based approach because we need the actual node objects to reconnect them. The technique is straightforward iteration: start at head, use a while loop to visit each node, store the node reference, and advance using next pointer. Time complexity is O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for storing n references. This feeds into L2 as the fundamental operation needed for the push phase. Common mistakes include storing node values instead of references, modifying nodes during traversal, or not handling the null terminator. This building block is essential for many linked list algorithms that require multiple passes or temporary storage of nodes.

Pseudocode:
function traverseAndStore(head):
    storage = empty list
    current = head
    while current != null:
        storage.append(current)  # Store node reference
        current = current.next
    return storage

examples.trace: Step 1: current points to node 1, store reference to node 1. Step 2: current moves to node 2, store reference to node 2. Step 3: current moves to node 3, store reference to node 3. Step 4: current becomes null, stop. Storage contains 3 node references.
Step 1: current points to node x, store reference to node x. Step 2: current becomes null, stop. Storage contains 1 node reference.

testCases.expected: Storage: [ref_to_100, ref_to_200, ref_to_300, ref_to_400, ref_to_500, ref_to_600]
Storage: [ref_to_9, ref_to_8, ref_to_7, ref_to_6, ref_to_5, ref_to_4, ref_to_3, ref_to_2, ref_to_1]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop with Node References
ladders.desc: Perform basic stack operations (push and pop) using node references as elements.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation involves using a stack data structure to store and retrieve node references (not just values). The intuition is that a stack's LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) behavior naturally reverses sequences: what goes in first comes out last. For linked list reversal, we push nodes in original order, then pop them to get reverse order. The key insight is that we're storing the actual node objects, so when we pop them, we have the exact nodes to reconnect. Basic operations: push(node) adds node to top of stack, pop() removes and returns top node. Time complexity for each operation is typically O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for storing n nodes. This feeds into L3 as the specific data structure used for storage. Common mistakes include confusing stack with other data structures, not handling empty stack pops, or pushing node values instead of references. This operation is fundamental to understanding how stacks can be used for sequence reversal in various algorithms.

Pseudocode:
# Initialize stack
stack = []
# Push operation
stack.append(node)  # Python list as stack
# Pop operation
if stack not empty:
    node = stack.pop()  # Removes and returns last element

examples.input: Push nodes A, B, C then pop three times
examples.trace: Step 1: Push A. Stack: [A]. Step 2: Push B. Stack: [A,B]. Step 3: Push C. Stack: [A,B,C]. Step 4: Pop returns C. Stack: [A,B]. Step 5: Pop returns B. Stack: [A]. Step 6: Pop returns A. Stack: []. Final pop order is reverse of push order.
Step 1: Push X. Stack: [X]. Step 2: Pop returns X. Stack: []. Single element behaves as expected.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Stack LIFO Behavior
ladders.desc: Learn how stacks work with Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle using simple examples.
ladders.explanation:
This is the foundational concept: understanding how a stack data structure works with its Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle. Imagine a stack of plates: you add plates to the top (push), and you remove plates from the top (pop). The last plate you put on is the first one you take off. This natural reversal is exactly what we use for linked list reversal. The intuition is simple: if you put items in order 1,2,3, they come out as 3,2,1. For beginners, visualize a vertical stack where push adds to top, pop removes from top. Time complexity for push/pop is O(1). Space complexity depends on number of items stored. This feeds into L4 as the theoretical basis for stack operations. Common mistakes include thinking stacks are FIFO (like queues), not understanding that pop removes the item, or confusing stack with other data structures. This foundation is crucial for understanding how the stack-based reversal approach works at its core.

Pseudocode:
# Simple stack operations
stack = []  # Empty stack
stack.append('A')  # Push A
stack.append('B')  # Push B
top_item = stack.pop()  # Pop returns 'B'

examples.input: Push: red, green, blue then pop
examples.trace: Step 1: Push red. Stack: [red]. Step 2: Push green. Stack: [red,green]. Step 3: Push blue. Stack: [red,green,blue]. Step 4: Pop removes and returns blue (last added). Stack: [red,green]. Last in, first out.
Step 1: Push 5. Stack: [5]. Step 2: Pop removes and returns 5. Stack: []. Single element works the same way.

testCases.input: Push: X, Y, Z, Pop three times
Push: morning, afternoon, evening, Pop three times
Push: first, second, third, fourth, Pop four times
